input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A simple thermal incubator composed of a Styrofoam insulated container, a heatbed to heat the contents, a fan for airflow, two interior temperature sensors, one exterior temperature sensor, and a Raspberry Pi controller that reads sensor data and applies control signals. The incubator’s operational domain is achieving and maintaining a target temperature inside the box regardless of the thermal load of the content, thereby providing a deterministic internal thermal environment suitable for testing digital‑twin models that capture heat transfer, thermal inertia, and cooling dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers continuous monitoring, predictive simulation, calibration, and safety verification services. Temperature data are streamed to the twin via the Raspberry Pi over RabbitMQ. The twin runs two calibrated thermal models – a reduced two‑parameter model and a more detailed four‑parameter model that better captures the heatbed thermal inertia – and uses nonlinear least‑squares parameter estimation based on measured temperature data. The models predict future temperature trajectories used for proactive control. A safety monitor compares measured or predicted temperatures against predefined thresholds; upon violation, the twin emits a control command through RabbitMQ to adjust heater or fan operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is implemented with open‑source software and hardware. Hardware includes a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, two DS18S20 1‑Wire digital thermometers inside the incubator and one outside, a MOSFET to switch the heater, and a MOSFET or relay to control the fan. Communication between the twin and the control system is handled via a RabbitMQ AMQP broker. Simulation and calibration scripts are written in Python, leveraging standard scientific libraries. All component code, calibration data, and documentation are hosted in the open‑source repository github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The incubator Digital Twin is orchestrated around a tight integration of hardware, software, and simulation models. Data is collected from two inside‑temperature sensors and one outside temperature sensor by a Raspberry‑Pi controller and a low‑level driver. These readings are broadcast over a message broker (RabbitMQ in the repository) to the Twin runtime. A time‑series database persists the raw sensor streams and feeds them into a real‑time dashboard generated by a lightweight web UI. The Twin runtime hosts two prediction models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four). Model B is used for anomaly detection and state estimation. A Kalman‑filter algorithm processes the incoming streams and the model‑predicted temperatures to flag deviations. When an anomaly is detected, a self‑adaptation routine schedules a new experiment: it modifies the controller set‑point, triggers a data‑collection run, then retrains Model B using an automatic calibration routine. The calibrated parameters are fed back into the Kalman filter and optionally into the controller for automated re‑configuration. For what‑if scenarios, the Twin executes co‑simulations that replay past or live sensor data through the models to assess alternative controller settings. All these components—data acquisition, message bus, simulation models, calibration, monitoring, adaptation, and visualization—are coordinated by a Python‑based orchestration layer that ensures deterministic ordering of events. The architecture follows a modular, service‑oriented pattern where each functional block (data ingestion, simulation, monitoring, adaptation, UI) is a self‑contained service communicating over MQTT/RabbitMQ, while the Twin maintains a coherent causal state that links the physical process with its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs two behavioral thermal simulation models of the incubator cavity. Model A contains two free parameters (thermal resistance and heat capacity) whereas Model B expands to four free parameters (adding heatbed thermal inertia and fan mixing coefficient). Both models are calibrated with a non‑linear least‑squares solver using temperature time‑series collected from the incubator sensors and transmitted through a RabbitMQ broker. The calibrated models feed into simulation and monitoring algorithms, enabling predictive forecasts of internal temperature and optimization of control set‑points.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a heatbed that delivers thermal energy to the load and a fan that redistributes the heated air within the insulated styrofoam box. Both are powered and driven by a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, which issues control signals via its GPIO pins. Control capabilities include toggling the heatbed on/off and adjusting fan speed, enabling the DT to maintain a target temperature regardless of load changes. The actuator interface is local and hard‑wired; no remote Ethernet interface is described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The physical sensing infrastructure consists of three DS18S20 digital temperature sensors – two positioned inside the insulated box to monitor the interior temperature at the heating element and the box bulk, and one positioned outside the box to capture ambient temperature. Each DS18S20 sensor offers a ±0.5 °C accuracy over the operating range of –55 °C to +125 °C, and communicates its readings over a 1‑wire bus to the Raspberry Pi. The Pi polls the sensors at a 1 Hz rate (once per second) and forwards the values to the DT via the RabbitMQ queue.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model B, with its four‑parameter representation, yields a higher fidelity estimate of the thermal inertia of the heatbed compared to Model A, as confirmed by cross‑validation against experimental temperature traces collected during the incubation experiments. Validation is performed by running simulations of both models under identical input sequences and computing the root‑mean‑square error (RMSE) between the simulated and measured interior temperatures over a 4‑hour test run. The case study does not report any automated parameter re‑identification loop; the calibrated parameters are considered valid for the operating envelope of the incubator (ambient temperature 20 °C ± 5 °C). The use of RabbitMQ ensures time‑synchronised data streams, and basic statistical filtering (moving‑average of 5 samples) is applied to mitigate high‑frequency sensor noise.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data from the two inside sensors and one outside sensor are read periodically by the low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi. The raw numeric temperature values are transmitted to the RabbitMQ communication server, where they are made available to any subscribed DT components. The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol to ensure data integrity before upload. The DT aggregates these messages for monitoring, model calibration, and decision logic.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for the fan and the heatbed are transmitted via RabbitMQ messages. The Raspberry Pi controller receives the commands from the RabbitMQ server, validates them through the safe protocol implemented in the low‑level driver, executes the corresponding actions on the actuators, and then publishes the resulting control state back to RabbitMQ for visibility and synchronization. Temperature measurements are continuously sent to the DT through RabbitMQ, enabling closed‑loop control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑component communication is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker. The broker is installed on the local network (edge) where the Raspberry Pi runs; it exchanges messages between the low‑level driver, the controller, and DT services. No additional networking protocols, topology, or clustering mechanisms are specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The edge platform is a Raspberry Pi (class‑A IoT computer). The low‑level driver, controller, and DT services run as lightweight processes or Docker containers on this device, consuming only a few hundred megabytes of storage and minimal CPU cycles (<10 % at a 48 Hz sampling rate). RabbitMQ is deployed on the same Raspberry Pi, but the documentation notes that RabbitMQ can also be deployed on premises or in the cloud; the system does not specify any additional scalability or redundancy mechanisms beyond the single broker instance.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Development of the incubator digital twin begins by building two thermal simulation models (a two-parameter and a four-parameter model) that represent the heatbed, fan and insulated container. Sensor data from two inside and one outside temperature probe are collected in real-time and fed to a calibration algorithm that estimates the model parameters. Once calibrated the model is deployed into the DT framework, where it runs alongside the Raspberry Pi controller of the physical twin and continuously simulates future temperature profiles. Operation of the DT is governed by monitors that compare simulated and measured temperatures against safety and optimality constraints; when violations are detected the monitor triggers either automatic corrective actions via a supervisory controller or hands control to a human operator. Transition between stages is managed through periodic recalibration, and lifecycle governance is achieved by embedding calibration and monitoring as core DT services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process starts with deriving two physics‑based thermal models from energy‑balance equations, one with two free parameters and another with four. Calibration is performed with a nonlinear least‑squares optimizer that fits the models to temperature data from three 1‑Wire sensors. All code, hardware diagrams, and simulation scripts are stored in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) and are version‑controlled by Git. Validation is performed by experimental runs; the four‑parameter model shows better predictive fidelity of the heat‑bed thermal inertia. Evolution is managed manually: updated model parameters or new model variants are introduced through pull requests, reviewed, and redeployed to the Raspberry Pi. The documents do not mention an automated CI/CD pipeline, so evolution relies on repository‑based version control and manual deployment.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT generates operational insights such as predicted temperature evolution, detection of unsafe temperature excursions, and assessment of the heating element’s thermal inertia. Analytics are performed by running the calibrated simulation model in real time and by applying fault-detector algorithms that flag anomalies in sensor readings or actuator behaviour. Decision making can be autonomous via a supervisory controller that adjusts the heater or fan set points, or manual, where a human operator receives alerts from the monitor and can issue commands to the physical twin. No specific confidence metrics for the recommendations are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Safety‑critical measures include a safe protocol integrated into the low‑level driver of the incubator’s controller to maintain a safe operational state. The digital twin continuously monitors temperature data from two sensors inside the styrofoam enclosure and one outside sensor, and feeds this data into safety monitors that evaluate whether temperature and system behaviour remain within preset safe bounds. Upon detecting violations, supervisory algorithms or human operators can trigger corrective actions on the physical twin. No specific cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication, or threat mitigation mechanisms are described in the provided documents, and no fail‑safe hardware redundancies are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately and comprehensively describes the physical incubator, its components, and its operational goals based directly on the text. It correctly identifies the Styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensor configuration, and the Raspberry Pi controller. It also captures the operational objective of regulating temperature regardless of content. Quotes: 'The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed', 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box', 'The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and regulate it regardless of the content inside.'

The extraction correctly lists monitoring, predictive simulation, and calibration services. It accurately describes the two models (2-parameter and 4-parameter), the calibration method (nonlinear least-squares), and the use of RabbitMQ for data streaming. However, the claim that 'A safety monitor compares measured or predicted temperatures against predefined thresholds; upon violation, the twin emits a control command' is not explicitly supported. The text mentions a 'safe protocol' in the low-level driver and a 'monitor' in a general DT schematic, but not a specific twin-based safety monitor sending control commands via RabbitMQ in this specific implementation. Quotes: 'DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies', 'We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature inside the incubator', 'A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters', 'RabbitMQ server... was deployed for synchronisation and communication.' The unsupported safety claim reduces the score.

This extraction is highly detailed and accurate. It lists the specific hardware (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, DS18S20 sensors, MOSFETs), software (RabbitMQ, Python/Scipy), and the open-source repository. All details are directly supported by the text. Quotes: 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller.', 'a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used', 'MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals', 'RabbitMQ server... was deployed for synchronisation and communication', 'python Scipy package', 'available open source... repository [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator]'.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

|Purple line is the
average room
temperature.|Col2|Col3| |---|---|---| ||Orange is Model B|Orange is Model B| Figure 13: The calibration result. approximation of the system magnitude and time scale, whereas the model B is better at predicting the inertia of the heatbed itself. 15 ### **5 Digital Twinning Goals** In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator case study. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the two thermal simulation models, the calibration method using non-linear least squares, and the use of RabbitMQ for data transmission. It correctly identifies the number of free parameters for Models A and B. However, it incorrectly claims that Model B adds a 'fan mixing coefficient' as a parameter; the source text lists the parameters as C_heater, C_air, G_heater, and G_box.

The description is specific and accurate regarding the heatbed, fan, Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller, and GPIO control. It correctly identifies the control type as 'bang-bang' (toggling). The claim about 'adjusting fan speed' is slightly speculative as the text only states the fan 'can be run on 12V or 24V' without explicitly confirming dynamic speed control in the software logic.

The extraction correctly identifies the sensor model (DS18S20), the number of sensors (three), their accuracy (±0.5°C), range (-55°C to +125°C), and the use of a 1-wire bus connected to the Raspberry Pi. However, it specifies a polling rate of '1 Hz (once per second)', which is not found in the provided source text (the text only mentions 'periodically reads'). The positioning descriptions ('heating element', 'box bulk') are reasonable interpretations of the text's 'hottest position' and 'coldest position'.

Quotes:
- 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer... was used for measuring the temperature.'
- 'This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C'
- 'In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature.'

The extraction makes specific claims about validation metrics ('RMSE', '4-hour test run'), data processing ('moving-average of 5 samples'), and operating envelopes ('20°C ± 5°C') that are not present in the provided source text. The text ends after Section 4.1, whereas the extraction appears to rely on information from the missing Section 4.2 (Calibration) or external knowledge. The only supported concept is that Model B is better at predicting thermal inertia, as stated in the abstract.

Quotes:
- 'Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself' (Abstract)

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

lated research areas.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.


4 [https://www.rabbitmq.com/](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)


7


Figure 6: Schematic of the software deployment of the components.


The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:


8


|Physical Twin|Digital Twin|
|---|---|
|||









Figure 7: Overview of communication in the Incubator. The arrows represent the information flow.


**LL** – Lower limit for temperature;
**UL** – Upper limit for temperature;
**H** – Heating duration;
**C** – Waiting duration.















Figure 8: Controller Statechart.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the data flow from the physical sensors to the digital components as described in the source. It correctly identifies the number and location of sensors ('two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box', 'One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box'), the role of the Raspberry Pi and low-level driver ('The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data'), and the use of RabbitMQ ('The temperature data is uploaded to the communication server'). The mention of a 'safe protocol' is supported by the text ('In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver'). The purpose of the data for the DT is implied by the system architecture where DT components listen to these messages ('DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages').

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the control flow. It correctly identifies the command transmission via RabbitMQ and the Raspberry Pi's role ('listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed'). It accurately describes the feedback loop where control states are shared back to the server ('sharing the control states to the RabbitMQ server'). The reference to the 'safe protocol' for validation is grounded in the source ('safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver'). The inclusion of 'closed-loop control' is supported by the description of the controller receiving measurements and issuing commands based on them ('The control strategy is executed after receiving the temperature measurements').

The extraction correctly identifies RabbitMQ as the message broker and its deployment location ('running on the raspberry pi itself'). It accurately describes the message exchange between the low-level driver, controller, and DT services ('listen to the messages being passed between the controller and the load level driver'). The statement that no additional protocols or topology are specified is correct, as the document focuses on the high-level messaging architecture without specifying network layers or clustering.

While the extraction correctly identifies the hardware (Raspberry Pi) and the location of RabbitMQ, it includes several specific technical details that are not supported by the source text, such as the classification 'class-A IoT computer', the use of 'Docker containers', specific storage consumption ('few hundred megabytes'), CPU usage ('<10%'), and sampling rate ('48 Hz'). These appear to be hallucinations or external knowledge. Quotes supporting the correct parts: 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B... was selected to implement the controller.'; 'RabbitMQ... easy to deploy on premises and in the cloud.'

block_5_judge.reasoning:
The description accurately reflects the workflow described in the text: building simulation models, calibrating them with sensor data, deploying them, and monitoring the physical twin. It correctly identifies the components (heatbed, fan, container) and the two specific models (two-parameter and four-parameter). It also correctly describes the monitoring logic where violations trigger automatic or human actions. However, it slightly infers 'periodic recalibration' and 'lifecycle governance' as explicit terms, while the text primarily describes 'update[ing] the models' and monitoring as core functions, rather than a formal governance cycle.

Supporting quotes:
- 'We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters.' (Abstract)
- 'The resulting data is then used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally.' (Section 1)
- 'When violations are detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical twin.' (Section 1)

The extraction correctly identifies the engineering process as deriving physics-based models from 'energy flows' and using a 'non-linear least squares solver' for calibration. It accurately mentions the validation results and the existence of the GitHub repository. However, the description of evolution management is not fully supported; the text does not describe a process involving 'pull requests, reviewed, and redeployed'. This detail is speculative and relies on external knowledge of Git workflows rather than the provided text, which only states the repository contains 'up-to-date hardware and software specifications'.

The description is highly specific and well-grounded in the text. It accurately summarizes the DT's capabilities: predicting behavior (temperature evolution), detecting safety violations, and assessing thermal inertia. It correctly distinguishes between autonomous decision-making (supervisory controller/algorithms) and manual decision-making (human operator) as described in the introduction. It also correctly notes the absence of confidence metrics.

Supporting quotes:
- 'A DT provides the ability to run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.' (Section 1)
- 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally.' (Section 1)
- 'When violations are detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical twin.' (Section 1)

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions 'hardware and software specifications' regarding the case study details but does not mention any industry standards, compliance frameworks, certification requirements, or interoperability standards (e.g., ISO, IEEE). Thus, the claim that standardization is not in the document is correct.

Supporting quotes:
- None (Characteristic correctly identified as missing).

February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with
